(born **Carole Ann Jones**; February 13, 1942 – September 3, 2019) was an American actress known for her roles in the films *[Blue Denim](/wiki/Blue_Denim "Blue Denim")* (1959) and *[The Poseidon Adventure](/wiki/The_Poseidon_Adventure_(1972_film) "The Poseidon Adventure (1972 film)")* (1972).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American actress (1942–2019)

| Carol Lynley | |
| --- | --- |
| Lynley in 1965 | |
| Born | Carole Ann Jones  (1942-02-13)February 13, 1942  New York City, United States |
| Died | September 3, 2019(2019-09-03) (aged 77)  Los Angeles, California, United States |
| Other names | Carolyn Lee |
| Occupations | * Actress * model |
| Years active | 1956–2006 |
| Known for | * *[The Potting Shed](/wiki/The_Potting_Shed "The Potting Shed")* * *[Blue Denim](/wiki/Blue_Denim "Blue Denim")* * *[The Poseidon Adventure](/wiki/The_Poseidon_Adventure_(1972_film) "The Poseidon Adventure (1972 film)")* * *[The Light in the Forest](/wiki/The_Light_in_the_Forest_(film) "The Light in the Forest (film)")* * *[Return to Peyton Place](/wiki/Return_to_Peyton_Place_(film) "Return to Peyton Place (film)")* * *[The Cardinal](/wiki/The_Cardinal "The Cardinal")* * *[Under the Yum Yum Tree](/wiki/Under_the_Yum_Yum_Tree "Under the Yum Yum Tree")* * *[Bunny Lake Is Missing](/wiki/Bunny_Lake_Is_Missing "Bunny Lake Is Missing")* |
| Spouse | Michael Selsman  ​  ​ (m. 1960; div. 1964)​ |
| Children | 1 |

**Carol Lynley** (born **Carole Ann Jones**; February 13, 1942 – September 3, 2019) was an American actress known for her roles in the films *[Blue Denim](/wiki/Blue_Denim "Blue Denim")* (1959) and *[The Poseidon Adventure](/wiki/The_Poseidon_Adventure_(1972_film) "The Poseidon Adventure (1972 film)")* (1972).

Lynley began her career as a child model before taking up acting. She won the [Theatre World Award](/wiki/Theatre_World_Award "Theatre World Award") as "one of the most promising personalities for 1956–57" for her performance in *[The Potting Shed](/wiki/The_Potting_Shed "The Potting Shed")*. Lynley started her film career in 1958 with the Disney film *[The Light in the Forest](/wiki/The_Light_in_the_Forest_(film) "The Light in the Forest (film)")*, followed by *[Holiday for Lovers](/wiki/Holiday_for_Lovers "Holiday for Lovers")* (1959) and *[Blue Denim](/wiki/Blue_Denim "Blue Denim")* (1959). In 1959, she was nominated for the [Golden Globe Award](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Awards "Golden Globe Awards") for [Most Promising Newcomer – Female](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_New_Star_of_the_Year_%E2%80%93_Actress "Golden Globe Award for New Star of the Year – Actress") for the film *The Light in the Forest*. A year later, she was again nominated for the same award for the film *Blue Denim*.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carol_Lynley&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Lynley was born Carole Ann Jones in Manhattan, New York City, the daughter of Frances (née Felch) and Cyril Jones. Her father was Irish and her mother, a native of [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England"), was of English, Scottish, Welsh and German ancestry. She studied dance in her childhood.[[1]](#cite_note-Hollywood_Reporter-1) Her parents divorced when she was a child, and her mother worked as a waitress until Lynley's income from modeling was enough to sustain the family.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Lynley had first appeared on a local television show and at the age of 14 she was signed as a child model. She then appeared on live TV shows, the *[Goodyear Television Playhouse](/wiki/Goodyear_Television_Playhouse "Goodyear Television Playhouse")*, *[Alfred Hitchcock Presents](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock_Presents "Alfred Hitchcock Presents")* and *[Danger Route](/wiki/Danger_Route "Danger Route")*.[[1]](#cite_note-Hollywood_Reporter-1)

She began her career as a child model under the name Carolyn Lee. When she started acting, she discovered that child actress Carolyn Lee (born Carolyn Copp, 1935)[[3]](#cite_note-Clarke-3) had already registered the name in the [Actors' Equity Association](/wiki/Actors%27_Equity_Association "Actors' Equity Association"). She modified it by using the final syllable of Carolyn and fusing it with Lee to make Lynley.[[4]](#cite_note-4) She appeared on the April 22, 1957, cover of *[Life](/wiki/Life_(magazine) "Life (magazine)")* identified as "Carol Lynley, 15, Busy Career Girl" at age 15.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

In her teenage years, Lynley appeared in several [Clairol](/wiki/Clairol "Clairol") and [Pepsodent](/wiki/Pepsodent "Pepsodent") advertisements that were publicized across the country.[[1]](#cite_note-Hollywood_Reporter-1)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carol_Lynley&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### Early career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carol_Lynley&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early career")]

In 1955, Lynley made her first stage appearance in [Moss Hart](/wiki/Moss_Hart "Moss Hart")'s [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre") stage hit *Anniversary Waltz*.[[1]](#cite_note-Hollywood_Reporter-1) At the age of 15, she played the role of Dame Sybil Thorndyke's granddaughter in the Broadway play *[The Potting Shed](/wiki/The_Potting_Shed "The Potting Shed")* (1957).[[1]](#cite_note-Hollywood_Reporter-1)

Early on, Lynley distinguished herself on both the Broadway stage and in [Hollywood](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_States "Cinema of the United States") screen versions of the controversial drama *[Blue Denim](/wiki/Blue_Denim "Blue Denim")* (1959), in which the teenaged characters played by Lynley and co-star [Brandon deWilde](/wiki/Brandon_deWilde "Brandon deWilde") had to deal with an unwanted pregnancy and (then-illegal) abortion.[[7]](#cite_note-7) She won the [Theatre World Award](/wiki/Theatre_World_Award "Theatre World Award") as "one of the most promising personalities for 1956–57" for her performance in *Blue Denim*.[[1]](#cite_note-Hollywood_Reporter-1) This recognition helped her get a seven-year contract with [20th Century Fox](/wiki/20th_Century_Fox "20th Century Fox").[[1]](#cite_note-Hollywood_Reporter-1)

### Film career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carol_Lynley&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Film career")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Carol_Lynley_as_Harlow.jpg/250px-Carol_Lynley_as_Harlow.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carol_Lynley_as_Harlow.jpg)

Lynley as [Jean Harlow](/wiki/Jean_Harlow "Jean Harlow") in 1965's *[Harlow](/wiki/Harlow_(Magna_film) "Harlow (Magna film)")*

Lynley started her film career in 1958 with the Disney film *[The Light in the Forest](/wiki/The_Light_in_the_Forest_(film) "The Light in the Forest (film)")* followed by *[Holiday for Lovers](/wiki/Holiday_for_Lovers "Holiday for Lovers")* (1959).[[8]](#cite_note-variety-8) In 1959, she was nominated for the [Golden Globe Award](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Awards "Golden Globe Awards") for [Most Promising Newcomer – Female](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_New_Star_of_the_Year_%E2%80%93_Actress "Golden Globe Award for New Star of the Year – Actress").[[9]](#cite_note-GoldenGlobe-9) In 1960, she was again nominated for the Golden Globe Award for Most Promising Newcomer – Female for the film *Blue Denim*.[[9]](#cite_note-GoldenGlobe-9)

She acted in 20th Century Fox productions *[Holiday for Lovers](/wiki/Holiday_for_Lovers "Holiday for Lovers")*, *[Blue Denim](/wiki/Blue_Denim "Blue Denim")*, *[Hound-Dog Man](/wiki/Hound-Dog_Man "Hound-Dog Man")* (all in 1959), *[Return to Peyton Place](/wiki/Return_to_Peyton_Place "Return to Peyton Place")* (1961) and *[The Stripper](/wiki/The_Stripper_(film) "The Stripper (film)")* (1963). *The Stripper* was based on the play *A Loss of Roses* written by [William Inge](/wiki/William_Inge "William Inge").[[1]](#cite_note-Hollywood_Reporter-1)

Lynley appeared in many films, often portraying the blonde-[girl-next-door](/wiki/Girl_next_door "Girl next door") gone bad.[[1]](#cite_note-Hollywood_Reporter-1) She is best known for her film roles in *[Return to Peyton Place](/wiki/Return_to_Peyton_Place_(film) "Return to Peyton Place (film)")* (1961), the sex comedy *[Under the Yum Yum Tree](/wiki/Under_the_Yum_Yum_Tree "Under the Yum Yum Tree")* (1963), the drama *[The Cardinal](/wiki/The_Cardinal "The Cardinal")* (1963), the romantic drama *[The Pleasure Seekers](/wiki/The_Pleasure_Seekers_(1964_film) "The Pleasure Seekers (1964 film)")* (1964), the thriller *[Bunny Lake Is Missing](/wiki/Bunny_Lake_Is_Missing "Bunny Lake Is Missing")* (1965) and *[The Poseidon Adventure](/wiki/The_Poseidon_Adventure_(1972_film) "The Poseidon Adventure (1972 film)")* (1972), in which she lip-synced the Oscar-winning song "[The Morning After](/wiki/The_Morning_After_(Maureen_McGovern_song) "The Morning After (Maureen McGovern song)")" (her singing voice was dubbed by studio singer Renee Armand).[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-BostonGlobe-11)

*[The Hollywood Reporter](/wiki/The_Hollywood_Reporter "The Hollywood Reporter")* reported that Lynley was on the peak of her career in the year 1965.[[1]](#cite_note-Hollywood_Reporter-1) She posed nude at age 22 for the March 1965 edition of *[Playboy](/wiki/Playboy "Playboy")* magazine.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) She starred in the [Otto Preminger](/wiki/Otto_Preminger "Otto Preminger") directed thriller *Bunny Lake Is Missing* (1965). Lynley played [Jean Harlow](/wiki/Jean_Harlow "Jean Harlow") in the biopic *[Harlow](/wiki/Harlow_(Magna_film) "Harlow (Magna film)")* (1965), co-starring [Ginger Rogers](/wiki/Ginger_Rogers "Ginger Rogers"), but the film failed at the box office.[[1]](#cite_note-Hollywood_Reporter-1)

The decline in her career started in the late 1960s. In 1967, Lynley had major roles in the horror film *[The Shuttered Room](/wiki/The_Shuttered_Room "The Shuttered Room")* and the British spy caper *[Danger Route](/wiki/Danger_Route "Danger Route")*, but the films were not successful. She did smaller roles, guest appearances and appeared in low-budget productions like *[Once You Kiss a Stranger](/wiki/Once_You_Kiss_a_Stranger "Once You Kiss a Stranger")* (1969), *[The Maltese Bippy](/wiki/The_Maltese_Bippy "The Maltese Bippy")* (1969), *[Norwood](/wiki/Norwood_(film) "Norwood (film)")* (1970) and the [Larry Hagman](/wiki/Larry_Hagman "Larry Hagman") directed horror spoof *[Son of the Blob](/wiki/Son_of_the_Blob "Son of the Blob")* (1972). In 1972, she made a brief comeback when she had supporting roles in *[The Night Stalker](/wiki/The_Night_Stalker_(1972_film) "The Night Stalker (1972 film)")*, a made-for-television film which drew top ratings, and *[The Poseidon Adventure](/wiki/The_Poseidon_Adventure_(1972_film) "The Poseidon Adventure (1972 film)")*, which was one of the top-grossing films of the year. However, she was unable to maintain her career momentum. She then appeared in *[The Four Deuces](/wiki/The_Four_Deuces "The Four Deuces")* (1975), *The Washington Affair* (1977) and *[Bad Georgia Road](/wiki/Bad_Georgia_Road "Bad Georgia Road")* (1977). In 1992, she acted in a low-budget thriller *Spirits*, as a nun. She acted in *Flypaper* (1997), followed by the low-budget film *Drowning on Dry Land* (1999).[[1]](#cite_note-Hollywood_Reporter-1) Many of the low-budget movies she acted in during the later part of her career were direct-to-video.[[11]](#cite_note-BostonGlobe-11)

She appeared in the pilot television movies for *[Kolchak: The Night Stalker](/wiki/Kolchak:_The_Night_Stalker "Kolchak: The Night Stalker")* and *[Fantasy Island](/wiki/Fantasy_Island "Fantasy Island")*.[[11]](#cite_note-BostonGlobe-11) Her many other series appearances include *[The Big Valley](/wiki/The_Big_Valley "The Big Valley")*, *[Mannix](/wiki/Mannix "Mannix")*, *[It Takes a Thief](/wiki/It_Takes_a_Thief_(1968_TV_series) "It Takes a Thief (1968 TV series)")*, *[Night Gallery](/wiki/Night_Gallery "Night Gallery")*, *[The Invaders](/wiki/The_Invaders "The Invaders")*, *[Kojak](/wiki/Kojak "Kojak")*, *[Hawaii Five-O](/wiki/Hawaii_Five-O_(1968_TV_series) "Hawaii Five-O (1968 TV series)")*, *[Tales of the Unexpected](/wiki/Tales_of_the_Unexpected_(TV_series) "Tales of the Unexpected (TV series)")*, *[Hart to Hart](/wiki/Hart_to_Hart "Hart to Hart")* and *[Charlie's Angels](/wiki/Charlie%27s_Angels "Charlie's Angels")*.[[8]](#cite_note-variety-8)[[14]](#cite_note-Movieweb-14) Lynley appeared in the fourth season of *[The Man from U.N.C.L.E.](/wiki/The_Man_from_U.N.C.L.E. "The Man from U.N.C.L.E.")* in the two-part episode "[The Prince of Darkness Affair](/wiki/The_Helicopter_Spies "The Helicopter Spies")".[[15]](#cite_note-15)

In 2000, in an interview with the *[San Francisco Chronicle](/wiki/San_Francisco_Chronicle "San Francisco Chronicle")*, Lynley discussed the difficulty faced by middle-aged actresses in finding roles. She predicted she'd have a comeback in old age, stating, "I don't mean to sound conceited, but I am a very talented actress, and I have my head screwed on right." And she added "I'm not going to drug clinics, I look good, and I've got all my marbles. So I really believe I'll be back."[[11]](#cite_note-BostonGlobe-11)

In 2006, she appeared in a 30-minute film, *[Vic](/wiki/Vic_(film) "Vic (film)")*, co-written and directed by [Sage Stallone](/wiki/Sage_Stallone "Sage Stallone"), the son of [Sylvester Stallone](/wiki/Sylvester_Stallone "Sylvester Stallone").[[8]](#cite_note-variety-8)[[14]](#cite_note-Movieweb-14)

## Personal life and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carol_Lynley&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Personal life and death")]

In 1960, Lynley married publicist Michael Selsman. The marriage produced one child, Jill Selsman (a director of short films), and ended in divorce in 1964.[[1]](#cite_note-Hollywood_Reporter-1)[[8]](#cite_note-variety-8) She also had an 18-year intermittent affair with English broadcaster and writer [David Frost](/wiki/David_Frost "David Frost").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Lynley died aged 77 of a heart attack on September 3, 2019, at her home in [Pacific Palisades, California](/wiki/Pacific_Palisades,_California "Pacific Palisades, California").[[1]](#cite_note-Hollywood_Reporter-1)[[8]](#cite_note-variety-8) She was cremated; her ashes were scattered at sea off the Southern California coast.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
